text.title: AI Search Optimization: llms.txt and Entity Strategy
text.summary: llms.txt standard for AI crawler guidance, entity optimization, and content structure for AI citation.

rule.statement: Create a /llms.txt file at the domain root that provides AI crawlers with a curated, structured overview of the site — similar to how robots.txt guides traditional crawlers.
rule.rationale: AI crawlers lack the context of traditional search crawlers. The llms.txt file provides the canonical summary of what the site is, what content it contains, and what should be excluded — reducing hallucination risk and improving citation accuracy.

list.item: Content: site description, core pages, product pages, contact, exclusions
Format: Markdown, starting with # sitename and a brief description line
Update: when site structure changes significantly

rule.statement: Use the exact same brand name, product names, and key personnel names across all pages, social profiles, and external mentions. Entity name inconsistency fragments knowledge graph representation.
rule.rationale: AI engines and Google's Knowledge Graph build entity representations from consistent co-occurrence patterns. Inconsistent naming ("Faion Network" vs "Faion" vs "faion.net") creates separate, weaker entity nodes instead of one authoritative entity.

list.item: Brand entity: Organization schema on every page, sameAs links to social profiles
Author entity: Person schema with sameAs links to LinkedIn, Twitter, GitHub
Product entities: consistent naming in all descriptions, meta, and schema
Relationship entities: explicitly link "Author X works at Organization Y" in schema
Clear entity definitions in the first paragraph of each page
Consistent entity naming throughout (no pronoun substitution for first mention)
Statistics attributed to named sources with publication date
Dates on all time-sensitive content (datePublished, dateModified in schema)
Author credentials visible near the article (name, role, expertise signal)
